title: [skip-ci] [CI:BUILD] Packit: Enable cockpit-podman tests on C10s
body:
[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED] - This is test configuration.

Suggested by @lsm5 . I enabled c9s/c10s in our [cockpit main builds COPR](https://copr.fedorainfracloud.org/coprs/g/cockpit/main-builds/)  recently, so *in theeeeory* this should work. :crossed_fingers: 

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

```release-note
None
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *lsm5*, *martinpitt* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [lsm5] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

[package installation still fails](https://artifacts.dev.testing-farm.io/97a02426-7a3a-429a-b09f-c8eb597ae82f/). There's something utterly wrong there, it installs packages from Fedora and Fedora tag repo into a CentOS image. That's just a hilarious mess. I need to adjust the repos.

looking better now but there are some installation conflicts. Don't know why it's fetching f40 packages.

```

Stderr:
---v---v---v---v---v---
Warning: Permanently added '3.15.184.207' (ED25519) to the list of known hosts.
Error: Transaction test error:
  file /usr/libexec/qemu-bridge-helper conflicts between attempted installs of qemu-kvm-common-18:9.1.0-2.el10.x86_64 and qemu-common-2:8.2.2-1.fc40.x86_64
  file /usr/share/man/man7/qemu-block-drivers.7.gz conflicts between attempted installs of qemu-kvm-common-18:9.1.0-2.el10.x86_64 and qemu-common-2:8.2.2-1.fc40.x86_64
  file /usr/share/man/man7/qemu-cpu-models.7.gz conflicts between attempted installs of qemu-kvm-common-18:9.1.0-2.el10.x86_64 and qemu-common-2:8.2.2-1.fc40.x86_64
  file /usr/share/man/man7/qemu-ga-ref.7.gz conflicts between attempted installs of qemu-kvm-common-18:9.1.0-2.el10.x86_64 and qemu-common-2:8.2.2-1.fc40.x86_64
  file /usr/share/man/man7/qemu-qmp-ref.7.gz conflicts between attempted installs of qemu-kvm-common-18:9.1.0-2.el10.x86_64 and qemu-common-2:8.2.2-1.fc40.x86_64
  file /usr/share/man/man1/qemu-kvm.1.gz conflicts between attempted installs of qemu-system-x86-core-2:8.2.2-1.fc40.x86_64 and qemu-kvm-common-18:9.1.0-2.el10.x86_64

```
I'll look up the dependency chain in podman tomorrow along with our copr and spec file.

@lsm5 Ah, that now smells very much like https://issues.redhat.com/browse/TFT-2564 , the gift that keeps on giving :cry: I'll see if we can work around this somehow.

[install log](https://artifacts.dev.testing-farm.io/ffbbbc9e-6074-4424-9118-ae97990c6580/guest-setup-6cf5ccbd-38a9-4b3d-809a-1af9ce9a46e8/artifact-installation-6cf5ccbd-38a9-4b3d-809a-1af9ce9a46e8/4-Install-packages.txt)

I filed https://github.com/cockpit-project/cockpit-podman/pull/1879 to try and attempt to work around this. I have to debug this through PRs, as it's difficult to locally reproduce the TF environment. I added a temporary commit here which tests against that branch instead of main -- once this works and that PR lands, I'll remove it again.
Cockpit tests failed for commit aa7c106527f4e0475205f85cadd837e771d75dc1. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit aa7c106527f4e0475205f85cadd837e771d75dc1. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.

Meh @thrix I need your help. -- I took out the declared cockpit-podman test dependency in https://github.com/cockpit-project/cockpit-podman/pull/1879, and this PR is running against that branch. I am trying to counterfight https://issues.redhat.com/browse/TFT-2564 to not install f40 packages in c10s, but the [test still fails](https://artifacts.dev.testing-farm.io/ed76f4d8-0702-4f45-9ea8-0d0d4f84d43b/) - the [package install log](https://artifacts.dev.testing-farm.io/ed76f4d8-0702-4f45-9ea8-0d0d4f84d43b/guest-setup-e1644f6a-f883-4e3c-ae0e-7a831e5d9440/artifact-installation-e1644f6a-f883-4e3c-ae0e-7a831e5d9440/4-Install-packages.txt) shows that it's installing "podman". This is *not* through a declared test dependency, but happens implicitly as part of "install all packages from the given copr". That fails as that pulls packages from F40 and then falls over. That happens at a point before the test script takes control.

Is there some other trick to run the `rm -f --verbose /etc/yum.repos.d/fedora.repo`? Maybe some "pre-deps-install:" step in [podman's plan](https://github.com/martinpitt/podman/blob/revdeps-c10s/plans/cockpit-podman.fmf) or [cockpit-podman's test declaration](https://github.com/cockpit-project/cockpit-podman/blob/fight-tf/test/browser/main.fmf)?

I did try the `adjust+` approach in the plan that Karel suggested in the Jira issue, but [it still fails](https://artifacts.dev.testing-farm.io/80e0b22a-d0ec-47c5-b7be-49211d9c0c39/) and I can't figure out why.

What is the point of testing centos stream here? My understanding was the we use the cockpit tests to catch podman regressions in our API, etc... I do not really see a high chance of a platform specific regression here.

We are consistently working to make our CI faster/less flaky and now we are looking at trim down a lot of the tasks from the PR context so adding more distros into the mix goes a bit against the direction we are currently moving.
cc @baude

@Luap99 yes, that's a good point, and why this needs to be weighed carefully. CentOS/RHEL 10 definitively get a ton of regressions, but we already spot them in cockpit-podman upstream. Tracking them in upstream podman PRs as well would be an additional burden for both of our teams. Maybe it's worth it, maybe not, I cannot say.

To clarify: from my POV this was a quick experiment, I'm not pushing much for this, or I'd also be happy to revert this if it turns out to not produce enough signal.

> What is the point of testing centos stream here?

I initially suggested that in response to a jira filed by @jelly about podman dependency issues causing failures in cockpit tests. But if cockpit can live with not having these in, fine by me too.

Ah, for things like https://issues.redhat.com/browse/RHEL-58240 ? Yes, we could have spotted such regressions with this test. Anyway, this is blocked until someone figures out how to get rid of this pesky fedora 40 repo on TF..

> Ah, for things like https://issues.redhat.com/browse/RHEL-58240 ? Yes, we could have spotted such regressions with this test. Anyway, this is blocked until someone figures out how to get rid of this pesky fedora 40 repo on TF..

That seems like are arguably small corner case, also if criu calls iptables that they need a dependency on it and cannot rely on other packages to pull it in. Also I doubt it would have been cought on a PR because that dep was in container-common/netavark rpm so there was never a podman PR that could have been blocked by that I think.

What I think is the far bigger issue is that nobody (well maybe besides you) will look at any of these failures anyway. The packit tests are way to often broken with bad mirrors or whatever else, there is absolutely no way that we can gate on these tests currently. As such adding them seems pointless for the most part, I agree that there is value to catch API regressions so one or two fedora test are fine (as long as someone looks at them).
Otherwise I see no reason why such regressions should not be caught in nightly runs outside of PRs were they do not block development.
Fair enough -- let's just shelve this then.
